title: Today's Markets Brief: Asia stocks retreat after fresh US strikes on Iran; PCE inflation on tap
summary: A quick look at the day's market news, with Asia stocks falling after US strikes on Iran and a look at 401(k) savings rates.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and this is your markets brief. Asia stocks are retreating today after fresh US strikes on Iran. The situation is being closely watched, and for now, it seems to be impacting market sentiment. The US PCE inflation data is also on tap for later today, which could give us more insight into the current state of the economy. I'm keeping an eye on how these events unfold, and it's interesting to see how markets react to geopolitical tensions.

According to a report from Investing.com, Asia stocks are indeed pulling back. This reaction seems to be driven by concerns over geopolitical tensions. Meanwhile, Fidelity's first-quarter data shows that while the number of 401(k) millionaires has decreased, workers are actually hitting record savings rates. This is an interesting dynamic, as it suggests that while some individual account balances may be dipping, overall savings behavior remains strong.

MarketWatch reports that Fidelity's data indicates a record savings rate, which is a positive sign. It seems counterintuitive that the number of 401(k) millionaires fell even as savings rates hit a high. My read is that it's a mixed bag – some accounts may have taken a hit, but overall, people are still prioritizing their retirement savings. I'm curious to see how this trend continues, especially with the PCE inflation data coming out today.

My quick read: today's market story has that familiar split-screen feeling. There is real excitement around artificial intelligence and chips, but the room still keeps one eye on risk, because markets enjoy a celebration more when they know where the exits are.
